content: When you are building CLI tools, the ProgressBar crate from indicatif is your default for rendering progress bars in terminals. It provides a high-level abstraction over terminal I/O that lets you draw styled progress indicators with minimal boilerplate. The core design decision here is to separate drawing logic from execution logic, which means you do not need to manually manage ANSI escape codes or buffer your output when calling the draw methods on your progress bar instances. This separation allows indicatif to be fast because it only updates the terminal state when necessary, rather than constantly polling for input or re-rendering every millisecond. You can create a simple ProgressBar instance with a total count and update its current value as you process data, but the real power comes from MultiProgress which lets you track multiple concurrent tasks without manually calculating offsets. When using multi-threaded workloads in Rust, each thread will spawn its own Progress Bar or MultiProgress instance unless you explicitly pass a shared handle to them; this prevents race conditions where one thread tries to write to the same file descriptor as another thread while they are updating their respective bars simultaneously. The memory implications of indicatif are surprisingly small because it does not allocate large buffers for every draw call; instead, it computes what needs to change and sends targeted updates to the terminal driver, which keeps your process footprint low even under heavy load. You should always use hidden progress bars when running long-running tasks in non-TTY environments like Docker containers or CI pipelines where a visual bar would look broken or confusing to users; you can disable this by setting the style to Hidden instead of Default, which prevents any drawing attempts and ensures your application continues smoothly without blocking on terminal output. For custom styles, indicatif allows you to create new Style objects that define colors for different parts of the bar such as the fill segment, the background, and the text itself; these styles are applied globally so if you change one part it affects all instances unless you explicitly override it per instance. The ETA estimation feature is particularly useful because it calculates elapsed time plus remaining work based on your current rate of progress, which gives users a realistic sense of how long their task will take without requiring them to manually track duration. From an expert perspective, the most important optimization in indicatif is avoiding unnecessary redraws by using the update method instead of calling draw repeatedly; this ensures that the terminal driver only processes changes when they actually matter, preventing performance degradation on slow terminals or high-latency connections. You will never need to implement your own progress bar logic unless you have very specific requirements beyond what indicatif already supports, because its architecture is designed specifically to handle the complexity of terminal rendering while keeping the API simple and ergonomic for everyday Rust developers.
